# Verify `--group-add` / `-G` on `sesam add` merges into an already-tracked
# secret's access list instead of replacing it, keeps the implicit admin group
# out of the persisted list, and rejects mixing both flags.

exec git init
exec git config user.email test@sesam.dev
exec git config user.name 'Sesam Test'

exec sesam init --user admin
exec git add -A
exec git commit -m 'init sesam'

# First add with an explicit group.
exec sesam add some_file.txt --group dev
exec sesam ls
stdout 'dev'

# Re-add with --group-add ops => this is a change_access that keeps dev.
exec sesam add some_file.txt --group-add ops
exec sesam show .sesam/audit/log.jsonl
stdout '"secret.change_access"'
exec sesam ls
stdout 'dev,ops'
exec sesam verify

# admin stays implicit: some_file.txt's persisted access is exactly dev+ops,
# with no admin entry leaking into the list (README keeps its own admin group).
grep '(?s)path: some_file.txt\n\s+access:\n\s+- dev\n\s+- ops' sesam.yml

# Passing both --group and --group-add is a usage error.
! exec sesam add some_file.txt --group dev --group-add ops
stderr 'mutually exclusive'

-- some_file.txt --
Some content
